title: How the System Works
subtitle: A guide to the indicators, the evaluation methodology, and the platform's features.

import.title: Supported Sites & Import
import.p1: Analysis starts from your hand history. Export the .txt file from your room and upload it, the system auto-detects the site and evaluates every decision. Supported rooms:
import.ps_where: Settings → Playing History → Hand History → Save My Hand History
import.gg_where: PokerCraft (in the client) → Hand History → Download Hand History
import.acr_where: Settings gear (top right) → Hand Histories → choose the folder
import.p2: You can upload multiple files at once. The same tournament is consolidated: when the site splits a long hand history into per-day files (same T#), the new hands are merged automatically.
import.p3: On ACR/WPN, the buy-in comes from the file name. For the prize and ROI, also upload the tournament results file (.ots format) so profit is computed correctly.
import.coin_where: PokerIntel → EV Chart → select date range → Download Hands

rating.title: ELO Rating System
rating.description: How ELO works, how we adapted it for poker, and how to read your rating.
rating.s1_p1: ELO is a rating system created by the Hungarian physicist Árpád Élő in the 1960s to rank chess players. Today it is used in chess (FIDE), tennis, football (FIFA), and many competitive games.
rating.s1_p2: The core idea: each player has a number (rating). When two players face off, the system computes the expected probability of each one winning based on the rating difference. After the match the winner gains points and the loser loses them, the more surprising the result, the more points change hands.
rating.s2_p1: With every analyzed decision, your rating rises or falls a little. The size of the adjustment depends on how surprising the result was relative to what's expected from someone at your level: playing well when you're already expected to moves it little; a serious mistake, or a hit above your level, moves it more.
rating.s2_p2: As in any ELO, the distance between two levels matters: a 400-point gap means roughly 10× more chance for the stronger side to win the matchup. That's why the rating neither spikes nor crashes from one tournament to the next.
rating.s3_p1: Poker has no direct opponent whose quality you can measure each match. We use a vs GTO Solver approach: each of your decisions is compared with the solver's solution. The platform's reference rating ("par") is 1500, the average player. High GTO adherence pushes your rating above par; serious mistakes push it below.
rating.s3_note: Important: ELO counts only decisions with GTO solver analysis. Spots without GTO coverage (not yet solved) are ignored, they neither inflate nor deflate your rating. This ensures the number reflects real adherence to equilibrium, not a heuristic estimate.
rating.s3_p2: How much each decision weighs in the rating depends on its alignment with GTO:
rating.q_mixed_desc: Played one of the actions the solver mixes in that spot
rating.q_minor_desc: Slightly off the ideal, but far from a serious mistake
rating.q_major_desc: Clear mistake, which costs the most in the rating
rating.s4_p1: As your rating rises, more is expected of you, so "obvious" hits are worth less and less, and mistakes weigh more. That mechanism stabilizes the number: climbing from one band to the next requires consistently higher GTO adherence, not just nailing the easy spots.
rating.n1: Adaptive volatility: the rating is more sensitive early on, to calibrate quickly, and stabilizes as you accumulate decisions, so isolated mistakes don't wreck an already consolidated rating.
rating.n2: Per-street rating: beyond the aggregate, we keep separate ratings for preflop / flop / turn / river. Useful to spot your strengths and weaknesses (e.g. ELO 2300 preflop but 1700 turn = a study opportunity).
rating.n3: Recomputed on each upload: after every new imported tournament, your ELO is recomputed and a new point is recorded on the evolution chart.
rating.n4: No public ranking: for now your ELO is private. When the leaderboard launches (opt-in feature), you decide whether you appear.

scoring.title: Scoring System
scoring.p1: Every imported decision receives a verdict (Correct, Acceptable or Error) that reflects how far it strays from the ideal play, taking into account hand strength (equity), bet sizing, position, and tournament context (M-ratio and ICM pressure).

table.correct_meaning: Solid line. Minimal deviation from optimal.
table.acceptable_meaning: Defensible action, but a slightly better alternative exists.
table.error_meaning: Relevant strategic loss: from small to costly. A spot to fix.

scoring.p2: The system factors in MTT context when reclassifying decisions: under high ICM pressure (short stacks or final table) error margins are tighter, an Acceptable decision can become an Error when the context amplifies the strategic cost.
scoring.p3: The Standard% of a tournament is the percentage of decisions labeled standard out of the total analyzed. It is the primary indicator of technical quality in a session.
scoring.example_standard: Correct fold of a weak hand OOP with insufficient pot odds, minimal deviation from optimal
scoring.example_marginal: Flop call with borderline equity, defensible action, but a slightly better alternative existed
scoring.p4: You see three verdicts, Correct, Acceptable and Error. Internally the system still measures the magnitude of each error (its EV cost): it does not change the verdict you see, but it feeds your leak ranking, study plan and rating. Indicators like Standard% and Clear Mistakes% measure that aggregate magnitude.
scoring.example_error: Value bet with poor sizing, or a river call with 20% equity against an 80%-pot bet, a relevant strategic loss to fix.

indicators.title: Indicators
indicators.std_measures: % of decisions classified as standard in the period
indicators.std_interpret: Target: above 70%. Elite players stay above 92%.
indicators.avg_measures: Average quality of your decisions in the period.
indicators.avg_interpret: Lower is better: low values indicate decisions consistently close to ideal; high values indicate a volume of mistakes.
indicators.clear_measures: % of decisions classified as a clear mistake (serious deviation from ideal).
indicators.clear_interpret: Directly linked to EV lost per session. Target: < 5%.
indicators.leakroi_measures: Estimated EV lost per leak spot in the period (in BBs)
indicators.leakroi_interpret: Prioritize leaks with the highest negative ROI, those cost the most.
indicators.icm_measures: ICM pressure level at decision time (low / medium / high)
indicators.icm_interpret: Incorrect decisions under high ICM cost more in the long run.
indicators.drift_measures: Quality decline trend across consecutive sessions
indicators.drift_interpret: Indicates tilt or fatigue. Reviewing recent sessions is recommended.
indicators.resultsgto_measures: Decisions that were CLEAR GTO mistakes but the hand was won (you collected the pot).
indicators.resultsgto_interpret: The higher it is, the more results are masking process errors, winning doesn't validate the play. Review these spots even when you win.
indicators.evloss_measures: How many big blinds you lost vs the best GTO play in that decision (preflop).
indicators.evloss_interpret: Turns "wrong" into "you leaked X bb": it quantifies the cost of the play. The higher, the more EV the decision left on the table; correct plays stay at 0. The Leak Finder panel ranks your spots by this total bb lost.
indicators.p1: Leak ROI combines how often each error spot occurs with the EV cost of getting it wrong there, prioritizing what weighs most over the long run. High-frequency low-cost spots can matter more than rare high-cost spots, and ROI captures that.

gto_method.title: GTO Classification Methodology
gto_method.p1: Each preflop decision is compared against the equilibrium (GTO) strategy for that exact situation, your table position, the effective stack depth, and the opponents' actions before you. When a GTO solution exists for the spot, it serves as the reference to evaluate your play; when no precomputed solution exists, the evaluation falls back to equity, bet sizing, and tournament context. Preflop analysis is done for MTT 9-max.
gto_method.rfi_condition: You're first to enter the pot, no one has bet before you
gto_method.rfi_range: Opening range for your position at your stack depth
gto_method.vs_rfi_condition: An opponent has already opened the pot and their position is known
gto_method.vs_rfi_range: Defense range for your position against the specific opener
gto_method.vs_3bet_condition: The pot has been 3-bet and the aggressor's position is known
gto_method.vs_3bet_range: 4-bet/call range for your position against that 3-bet
gto_method.quality_title: How your action's quality is classified
gto_method.correct_meaning: GTO-aligned action: hand in range and recommended action taken
gto_method.correct_impact: Keeps the best possible verdict for the hand
gto_method.acceptable_meaning: Defensible action: hand in range but a secondary action
gto_method.acceptable_impact: Neutral verdict: defensible, but not optimal
gto_method.p2: The system does not display preflop GTO analysis when it can't identify the opponent's position in the hand history, or when the pot is a 3-bet without an identified opener. In these cases the hand review shows only the postflop analysis (equity + pot odds). Known SB limitation: at some stack depths the model only covers fold/raise for the SB. In those spots, completing (limping) is treated as acceptable, not a mistake. Open size: when the opponent opens larger than the standard GTO size, the defense range shown is the one vs a minimum open; against a larger open the correct defense is tighter, so folding a marginal hand is treated as defensible, not a Error, and the card notes the oversized open.
gto_method.p3: System guarantee: we never display contradictory recommendations. The verdict shown on the main dashboard and in the hand review is always the same, both views are kept in sync automatically.
gto_method.coverage: Coverage: most standard preflop decisions receive a GTO verdict; what's left out are situations with no reference solution, limped pots (when nobody raises) and non-standard lines, which appear without a GTO classification, and count neither as correct nor as a mistake.
gto_method.error_meaning: Deviation from GTO: from calling with a hand outside the defense range to raising/calling a very weak hand against a strong range.
gto_method.error_impact: The verdict now flags an error to fix (the deviation magnitude feeds the leak ranking).

replayer.title: Hand Review (Replayer)
replayer.p1: The Replayer replays any hand step by step and shows the GTO analysis of each of your decisions. Use the controls to step forward and back, change speed, and switch the display between chips and big blinds (BB).
replayer.p2: On the table, each player shows their position (UTG, LJ, HJ, CO, BTN, SB, BB), stack, and the dealer button, so you can read the spot without counting from the button. Your cards are highlighted at the bottom.
replayer.p3: The decision card shows the GTO strategy for your exact cards, not the position's aggregate range. For example, a hand outside the opening range shows Fold 100%; a strong hand shows something like Raise 74% · All-in 26%. Alongside it are your verdict and the supporting indicators (equity, SPR, stack, ICM).
replayer.p4: When there is no reference GTO solution, the review is honest and does not fabricate a verdict. Limped pots appear tagged “{position} vs Limp” (a tree outside coverage), and option checks in uncontested pots are not evaluated.
replayer.p5: The equity indicator on the card is range-aware: when you face a known open, it is estimated vs the opponent's actual GTO opening range (labeled «vs range»), usually lower than vs a random hand, since that range is stronger than average. In other spots it falls back to vs a random hand (labeled «vs random»). Either way it is a strength reference; the verdict always comes from your hand's GTO strategy, not from this number.
replayer.p6: In multiway pots (3 or more players in the pot), the card shows a «Multiway · N-way» tag. The solver strategy is solved heads-up, so with 3+ players it is no longer reliable, and the card does not show it. Instead, the verdict comes from an independent estimate: your hand's equity vs the opponents' continuing range (the hands they bet or call with), weighed against the pot odds and adjusted for how many opponents are contesting the pot. When that read is clear, for example, calling a bet with equity below what you need, or failing to value-bet a strong hand, it is the verdict (tagged «Multiway»); on close decisions the card falls back to the engine's overall read. It is an honest estimate, not the exact solution of a multiway solver.
replayer.p7: When you bet or raise on the flop, turn, or river, the card shows the intent of the bet: Value bet (your hand is strong and you want worse hands to call), Value + protection (your hand is ahead, but the board can give your opponent a draw, the bet charges them to continue), Semi-bluff (you don't have a made hand yet, but you have a draw that can complete), Bluff (your hand is behind and you want a better one to fold), or Medium strength, a hand good enough to beat a bluff at showdown, but too weak to bet for value. Betting a medium-strength hand is usually a mistake: worse hands fold and only better hands call, so you only get called when you're behind. In those cases the card turns red and GTO prefers to check. It's the simplest way to see whether your bet had a clear goal, extracting value or forcing a fold, or whether it was betting just to bet.

pko_tournaments.title: PKO (Progressive Knockout / Bounty) Tournaments
pko_tournaments.p1: The system automatically detects PKO tournaments. When detected, a PKO badge appears on the hand review and the analysis is adjusted, the bounty incentivizes aggression and lowers the equity required to stack off.
pko_tournaments.required_classic: Standard (requires more equity to risk the stack)
pko_tournaments.required_pko: Reduced (the bounty lowers the required equity)
pko_tournaments.final_pko: Stays high (the bounty effect sustains aggression)
pko_tournaments.p2: The PKO adjustments reflect the main qualitative shift in bounty theory: the equity required to stack off drops and aggression is rewarded more than in an equivalent classic MTT. Across all preflop decisions, opening (RFI), defending against an open, squeezing, and responding to re-raises (3-bet, 4-bet, facing a squeeze), the system consults PKO-specific GTO ranges, wider than those of an equivalent classic MTT, reflecting the bounty reward.

mstacks.title: M-Ratio Phases
mstacks.p1: Harrington's M measures how many full blind orbits (SB + BB + antes) your stack can sustain without playing a hand. It governs MTT strategy: the lower the M, the narrower the range of viable actions.
mstacks.deep_strategy: Full post-flop poker. Room for bluffs, slowplays, and stack maneuvers.
mstacks.mid_strategy: Pre-flop playability starts narrowing. Careful with big-stack confrontations.
mstacks.short_strategy: Focus on reshove/push-fold spots. Resteals important to maintain M.
mstacks.pushfold_strategy: Binary strategy: fold or all-in. ICM pressure automatically high.
mstacks.p2: Decisions in the Push/Fold phase are evaluated with criteria specific to that binary context (fold or all-in), where the required equity and position carry different weight than in the other phases.
mstacks.p3: Tournament stage (early, middle, late, final table, heads-up) is identified automatically and complements M in defining the context.

dna.title: Strategic Profile
dna.p1: Strategic Profile is the player's strategic fingerprint, a 5-axis radar calculated from the complete decision history. It reveals structural game patterns independent of financial results.
dna.aggression_calc: % of aggressive actions (bet/raise) out of total post-flop
dna.fold_calc: % of decisions that resulted in a fold
dna.threebet_calc: % of pre-flop situations where a 3-bet occurred vs opportunities
dna.threebet_ref: Varies by position; > 12% in EP may be exploitable
dna.positional_calc: Aggression difference between late position and early position
dna.discipline_calc: % of decisions classified as standard (= Standard%)
dna.p2: The combination of the axes produces an archetype: Nit, Calling Station, LAG, TAG or Balanced. The archetype is a diagnostic starting point, not a definitive label.

training.title: Training Mode (Leak Trainer)
training.p1: Training Mode turns your real leaks into focused practice, with instant correction against GTO. It's a safe environment: unlike real play, training never touches your real rating. It's where you rehearse before taking it to the table.
training.journey_title: The journey has three steps, in order:
training.step_train_meaning: Practice the spots behind your costliest leaks. Starts with fundamentals and gets more demanding as you improve.
training.step_play_meaning: Take what you trained to the real table: import and play your actual tournaments.
training.step_validate_meaning: Compares your real GTO adherence BEFORE vs AFTER training, proving that practice turned into results at the table.
training.p2: You choose what to train: adaptive mode (the system prioritizes your open leaks), by specific leak, or fundamentals (opening/RFI, BB defense, vs 3-bet).
training.tiers_title: Each spot category builds a mastery level as you get them right:
training.tier_bronze_meaning: Getting started, still misses this kind of spot often.
training.tier_silver_meaning: Gets most right, but the pattern isn't consolidated yet.
training.p3: If you stop practicing, mastery decays over time (spaced repetition), flagging what needs a revisit. Daily missions are short goals for the day that reset at midnight in your timezone, and a badge tells you when there's a pending lesson.
training.p4: Two-axis principle: training is practice, play is competition. Training prepares you, but only real play moves your ELO, the two never mix.

ghost.title: Ghost Table / Drills
ghost.p1: The Ghost Table converts your own leaks into review drills. Each "spot" is a recurring pattern where you made below-optimal decisions, the system extracts these spots automatically from your imported history.
ghost.p2: Spaced repetition: after each drill session, the interval until the next review is adjusted based on your performance:
ghost.hit_next: The interval until the next review grows with each hit.
ghost.miss_next: A miss restarts the spot at the minimum interval.
ghost.mastery_next: A mastered spot is archived at the maximum interval.
ghost.p3: The drills card on the dashboard shows spots with overdue review (red), about to expire (yellow), and on track (green). The goal is to keep all spots green, a sign that the patterns have been internalized.
ghost.p4: XP earned: 25 XP per completed drill, 100 XP for reaching mastery on a spot.
ghost.p5: Pressure Mode adds a 30-second countdown per decision. When the timer reaches zero, a fold is automatically recorded and the hit streak resets. The streak is shown with a 🔥 badge during the session and on the final summary.
ghost.validation: Every answer is validated against the GTO Solver. When your exact hand's analysis exists, grading uses ITS strategy ("Solver · your hand" badge); otherwise the range strategy for the spot ("Solver · range"). Without solver coverage, validation falls back to the engine heuristic, and the amber badge makes that explicit. Plays inside the solver's mix count as correct (mixed line); outside the mix, error.
ghost.variety: Spots come from YOUR mistakes (not random hands), prioritized by severity and spaced repetition (SRS), and each session varies spot types so you don't repeat the same situation ten times.
ghost.streetFocus: You can also focus a session on a specific street (preflop, flop, turn or river). Handy when an indicator shows you're bleeding on one street: you review and drill just those mistakes, on repeat, until you understand where you went wrong and how you should have played.

compare.title: Tournament Comparison
compare.p1: Select 2 to 4 tournaments on the Tournaments page and click "Compare" to see a side-by-side analysis. The comparison shows:
compare.bullet_standard: Standard% and Avg Score for each tournament
compare.bullet_leaks: Top 3 active leaks per session with frequency and EV loss
compare.bullet_narrative: AI-generated narrative highlighting technical evolution or regression
compare.p2: The Delta (▲/▼) shows the change in an indicator between the reference tournament (oldest selected) and the others. Green means improvement, red indicates regression in the same spot.

coaching.title: Coaching
coaching.p1: When a coach is linked to your account, the system automatically creates a baseline, a snapshot of your indicators at the time of linking. All tournaments imported after that are compared against this baseline to measure real progress.
coaching.baseline_meaning: Average Standard%, Avg Score, and top leaks at the coaching start date
coaching.delta_meaning: Indicator changes since baseline, the primary progress metric
coaching.reviewed_meaning: The coach annotated or commented on at least one decision in the session
coaching.override_meaning: The coach replaced the AI-generated study plan with a custom one
coaching.effectiveness_meaning: Median Standard% improvement for students in the first 60 days of coaching
coaching.p2: Coach effectiveness measures the improvement in students' Standard% since the coaching began. Coaches with 3 or more students under coaching receive a verified public badge in the directory.

gamification.title: Gamification
gamification.p1: The XP system rewards study activity, not financial results. Your XP is saved and follows you on any device.
gamification.event_drill: Get a drill right in the Ghost Table (once per day per spot)
gamification.levels_title: Level is computed from your ELO rating (GTO adherence, recent form):
gamification.streak_desc: The number of consecutive days with registered activity (any XP-generating event). Resets if a day passes without activity.
gamification.ach_first: First Analysis: import and analyze the first tournament
gamification.ach_drill: First Drill: complete the first drill in the Ghost Table
gamification.ach_streak: Week of Focus: 7 consecutive days of streak
gamification.ach_top10: Top 10: reach the top 10 of the students leaderboard
gamification.ach_top3: Podium: reach the top 3 of the leaderboard
gamification.ach_first_place: No. 1: reach 1st place on the leaderboard
gamification.ach_climber: Climber: move up in the leaderboard
gamification.ach_expert: GTO Expert: reach the Expert band in the ELO rating
gamification.levels_note: The ELO band and the GTO adherence % are two ways of viewing the same level: ELO is the rating number (it rises and falls with your play); adherence is what it means in practice (how often you match the solver line).

ranking.title: Students Leaderboard
ranking.p1: The Students Leaderboard ranks by learning, GTO adherence, improvement, engagement and study volume, and not by money. The goal is to give visibility to those who study consistently, not those with the biggest bankroll. The ranking is based on your ELO rating (see the ELO Rating page).
ranking.privacy: Appearing on the public leaderboard is optional: by default you don't appear. You turn participation on whenever you want and can pick a handle to compete without exposing your username. Your coach always sees your numbers, regardless of this setting.
ranking.position: Even without joining the public leaderboard, you always see your position and a change indicator (▲ up, ▼ down, ▬ steady) versus the previous record.
ranking.hof: The Hall of Fame highlights who was No. 1 each month. Champions without public participation appear as anonymous.
ranking.coach: Coaches see a ranking of their own students only, with real names, a follow-up view, with no competition between coaches.
ranking.note: Leaderboard achievements (Top 10, Podium, No. 1, Climber, GTO Expert) are added to your achievements and trigger a notification when unlocked.

career.title: Career Trajectory
career.p1: The Career Trajectory card projects when you'll reach each level based on your real ELO curve across imported tournaments. The projection starts from your recent improvement trend to estimate the dates.

table.slope_meaning: ELO improvement per tournament (e.g. +3 = you gain ~30 ELO every 10 tournaments)
table.milestone_meaning: ELO threshold to reach each level (e.g. Sólido requires 1816 ELO)
table.blocking_meaning: High-frequency leak spots that most impact your current ELO

career.p2: The dashed line in the chart represents the future projection, not a guarantee. If the slope is negative or zero, the system indicates stagnation and does not project dates.
career.p3: The AI narrative in the card summarizes: (1) current trend, (2) estimated time to the next level, (3) the priority leak to fix to accelerate progression.

cognitive.title: Cognitive Patterns
cognitive.p1: The Cognitive Patterns detects cognitive-emotional patterns in the chronological sequence of decisions within each tournament. Instead of looking at isolated decisions, the system tracks the sequence of your plays to identify when your mental state shifts.

patterns.revenge_trigger: A tendency to force aggressive plays after a run of disciplined folds.
patterns.revenge_signal: Aggression mistakes that surface right after that emotional trigger.
patterns.fear_trigger: Over-retreating after taking a few aggressive mistakes in a row.
patterns.fear_signal: Misguided folds driven by fear rather than the hand itself.
patterns.sunk_trigger: Attachment to what's already been invested in the pot over the hand.
patterns.sunk_signal: Wrong calls in sequence just to avoid giving up the chips already in.
patterns.entitlement_trigger: Overconfidence after a strong run of correct decisions.
patterns.entitlement_signal: A drop in discipline and mistakes right after a very positive stretch.
patterns.compensation_trigger: An urge to compensate for a disciplined fold of a strong hand.
patterns.compensation_signal: Undue calls trying to win back the action you let go.
patterns.icm_trigger: High-ICM pressure ignored at decisive final-table moments.
patterns.icm_signal: Risking the stack instead of tightening when ICM called for caution.

cognitive.p2: Each pattern receives a frequency (% of opportunities where the pattern manifests) and a severity level (high, medium or low). The card needs a minimum volume of decisions to activate.
cognitive.p3: The AI narrative describes the dominant pattern, why it costs EV, and one concrete corrective habit to apply in the next session.

twin.title: Strategic Patterns
twin.p1: The Strategic Patterns turns your spot-level error patterns into predictive language. Instead of listing past mistakes, the system says: "in shove preflop spots under high ICM, you make errors 63% of the time, well above your personal average of 28%".
twin.p2: Each spot is defined by the combination of street (preflop/flop/turn/river), correct action (fold/call/raise/bet/check/shove) and ICM pressure (low/medium/high/critical). The system highlights spots with a sufficient sample where you make errors above your personal average.

table.avg_meaning: Global percentage of decisions classified as an error (small mistake or clear mistake) in the last few months
table.costly_meaning: Spot with a sufficient sample where you make errors above your personal average
table.delta_meaning: How much your error rate in the spot exceeds (positive) or falls below (negative) your personal average

twin.p3: The vertical line in the bar chart represents your personal average. Bars that exceed this line indicate spots where you perform below your usual standard. The AI narrative summarises the 3 most costly tendencies and proposes one concrete adjustment.

leaks.title: Top Leaks Detected
leaks.p1: The Top Leaks card lists the most frequent and costly error patterns found in your history. A leak is a spot (combination of street, position and action) where you consistently make sub-optimal decisions, not an isolated mistake, but a habit.
leaks.p2: Each leak shows the frequency (how many times the pattern was detected), the average error severity, and the estimated EV lost per month if the behaviour continues. Leaks marked as Critical are the most serious and recurring errors, the ones that cost the most real ROI.
leaks.p3: The trend indicator (Improving / Stagnant / Regressing) shows whether your behaviour in that spot has improved over your recent tournaments. A Regressing leak needs immediate attention, it means you have reverted to the same mistake after a better period.
leaks.p4: Use the Study button to open the spot's drill directly in the Ghost Table. Completing the drill and repeating until mastery is the most effective way to internalise the correction.
leaks.example: Real detected leak: 'River overcall OOP', 31 occurrences, classified as Critical, trend Regressing. The estimated EV lost represents the monthly cost if the pattern continues uncorrected.

causal_map.title: Leak Causal Map
causal_map.p1: The Causal Map visualises which leaks co-occur in the same tournaments, across different hands of the same session. Each node is a leak; each line connecting two nodes indicates they appear together in the same tournament more often than expected. The line thickness represents the strength of the correlation.
causal_map.p2: Why it matters: when two leaks co-occur with high correlation, they usually share a root cause, for example, excessive preflop folding and bad flop calls may both stem from the same range-reading problem. Fixing the root cause resolves both at once.
causal_map.p3: Click a node to highlight only the leaks connected to it. Red nodes are critical; yellow are moderate; green are minor. Always start with the red nodes that have the most connections, they have the greatest cascading impact.

streets.title: EV Lost by Street
streets.p1: The Performance by Street card shows how much EV (in big blinds) you lose on each street: preflop, flop, turn and river. The smaller the bar, the better.
streets.p2: Streets with orange or red bars indicate where your leaks are concentrated. Most players lose the most on the river, where the pot is largest and mistakes are most expensive. Players who level up quickly typically fix preflop first (highest decision volume) and then the turn.
streets.p3: Use this card to prioritise which streets to focus on in the Ghost Table and Academy.
streets.example: Common pattern: the loss is small on the preflop (0.8bb) and grows toward the river (4.0bb). The river being the most expensive points to trouble with showdown decisions, fold equity and thin value.

positions.title: GTO Alignment by Position
positions.p1: The Performance by Position card shows your GTO alignment percentage in each table position, from the early seats (UTG) to the blinds (SB and BB).
positions.p2: Late positions (BTN, CO) naturally have more positional advantage, better performance is expected from those seats. If BTN or CO appear red, that is a serious issue: you are not taking advantage of the positional edge you have.
positions.p3: BB and SB defensive spots are technically the hardest. Poor performance in the BB is common and not necessarily a crisis, but if the delta between BTN and BB is very large, the positional deficit is costing real EV.
positions.example: BTN (84%) and CO (79%) make good use of positional advantage. BB (63%) and SB (60%) weaker, a 24pp delta between BTN and SB is elevated but expected. Above 30pp indicates a critical positional leak.

icm.title: Chips aren't money: ICM basics
icm.p1: In a cash game every chip is real money, always the same. In a tournament it isn't. You traded money for chips at sign-up, but you only turn them back into money if you finish in a paid spot. That leads to a rule that feels strange at first: a chip you win is worth less than a chip you lose. Winning chips inches you toward the prize; losing them all knocks you out in one shot. This imbalance has a name, ICM, and it's what separates players who understand tournaments from players who only play chips.
icm.p2: A quick example. Four players left, three get paid, and you have the biggest stack by far. If you risk it all in a spot that's a small chip profit and lose, you bust in 4th for nothing, even though you were the leader. If you pass on the risk, you probably reach the money just letting the others fight. Here, doubling your chips barely changes your prize, but busting destroys it. So sometimes the right play is to give up a chip profit to protect your place in the prizes.
icm.p3: In practice this adds one demand: near the money, only risk your stack when you're much more sure than you'd need to be early on. A hand that would be an easy call counting chips can become a fold once each bust-out moves your money. And it isn't only the bubble: ICM already weighs in once a good chunk of the field is gone (around half) and keeps growing toward the final table.
icm.p4: The two classic beginner mistakes are opposites: playing too tight too early, when ICM doesn't matter yet and you just needed chips; and calling too wide on the bubble, when surviving was worth more than being right. In GrindLab, when you fold a hand that the chip math would approve but that makes sense to fold for ICM, the card doesn't flag a mistake: it shows the ≈ chipEV approximation seal. That's the sign that our chip-based read falls short there, and your fold is defensible.

table.early_effect: ICM barely weighs. Build chips and take profitable spots; folding out of fear here is what costs money.
table.mid_effect: ICM starts to count. Think twice before putting your whole stack on the line.
table.bubble_effect: The tensest moment. Pressure medium stacks (they have more to lose) and avoid clashing with anyone who can bust you.
table.ft_effect: Each spot pays much more than the last. Surviving is worth gold, be selective.

icm.example_label: How much 'certainty' you need to call an all-in
icm.example: Counting only chips, calling an all-in that needs about a 40% chance to win is already a profit. Near the money, the same call usually needs around 50%, because busting is costly. Those ~10 extra points are the price of surviving. Below that, fold, even if it looks like a fair call.

pressure.title: Pressure Collapse
pressure.p1: The Pressure Collapse card compares the quality of your decisions across four ICM pressure contexts: no pressure, low pressure, medium pressure and high pressure. The closer to the bubble and final table, the higher the pressure.
pressure.p2: A technically solid player maintains the same score regardless of pressure. A delta above 8 points between high pressure and no pressure is a sign of collapse, you make significantly more mistakes when real money is at stake.
pressure.p3: Pressure collapse is one of the most expensive leaks in MTTs because it is precisely in high-pressure situations (bubble, FT) that decisions have the greatest monetary impact. If the card shows collapse, prioritise drills with high ICM context in the Ghost Table.

table.none_meaning: Early tournament: small blinds, no immediate elimination pressure
table.low_meaning: Tournament in progress, outside bubble, decisions carry normal weight
table.medium_meaning: Near the bubble or with significant pay jumps
table.high_meaning: On the bubble, final table, or critical reshove situation

pressure.example: Left: only a 4pp delta between no-ICM and high-ICM, consistent performance, a sign of solidity. Right: an 18pp delta, mistakes spike significantly on the bubble and final table, where each chip has greater monetary value.

bankroll.title: Bankroll Evolution
bankroll.p1: The Bankroll Evolution chart shows the cumulative financial result curve tournament by tournament. The line rises on profitable results and falls on losses, the overall shape reveals your long-term trend.
bankroll.p2: Financial results are heavily influenced by variance in MTTs, especially with samples below 100 tournaments. A downward curve does not necessarily mean you are playing poorly; it may be variance. That is why the system uses Standard% as the primary quality indicator, it measures decisions, not results.
bankroll.p3: Use the period filters (7 days, 30 days, 6 months, 1 year, All) to isolate trends in specific windows, and the By time / By tournament toggle to view the evolution by play date (on the calendar) or by tournament sequence. If your Standard% is rising but the financial curve is falling, you are playing better; results tend to catch up with volume.
bankroll.p4: If both Standard% and the curve are falling simultaneously, review your most recent leaks and check whether you have recently changed stakes, buy-in changes alter field competitiveness.

today.title: Today Summary
today.p1: At the top of the dashboard, the Today block sums up where you stand right now in three numbers: how much EV you lose, how solid your decisions are, and what your most expensive mistake is.
today.p2: EV lost /100 is the lead number: how much EV (in big blinds) you lose, on average, every 100 decisions. The lower, the better. The arrow shows whether it is improving or worsening versus the previous period.
today.p3: Solid decisions is the share of your decisions that follow a solid line (correct or acceptable). Train the most expensive leak points to your costliest current mistake (the action you took, the recommended one, the street and how much it cost in bb) and takes you straight to training.

kpis.title: ROI, ITM and Volume
kpis.p1: Just below the summary, three result indicators: ROI (return on the buy-ins you invested), ITM (how often you finish in the money), and Volume (how many tournaments and hands the sample covers).
kpis.p2: With a small sample, ROI becomes Net profit (in value), because a percentage over few tournaments is misleading. Volume is the context: the larger the sample, the more reliable the other numbers are.

coverage.title: GTO Coverage
coverage.p1: The GTO Coverage card shows, in two rings, what percentage of your decisions was compared against the GTO Solver reference: one ring for preflop and one for postflop.
coverage.p2: Preflop is usually covered almost immediately. Postflop grows over time, as the solver works through the spots in the background; that is why, when it is still below the total, the card shows the "under analysis" note. The higher the coverage, the more of your game has been checked against GTO.
coverage.p3: Multiway pots (3+ players): there is no reference GTO solution for pots with three or more players, solvers only solve heads-up. Instead of inventing a GTO number, your hand's equity in these spots is estimated by simulation (Monte Carlo) against the opponents' likely continuing ranges, adjusted for pot odds and a realization penalty (multiway you realize less of your equity than heads-up). That is why these spots are flagged as ≈ Approximation: an honest estimate, not a GTO verdict, and it counts neither as correct nor as a mistake.
